g.data-narration: Welcome to Risk Evaluation in Software Engineering - a critical skill for successful project management.
Nearly 50% of software projects exceed budgets and 30% are canceled. Risk evaluation helps prevent these failures through proactive planning.
A risk is an uncertain event that positively or negatively affects project objectives like scope, schedule, cost, and quality.
g.text: An uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect
on at least one project objective such as scope, schedule, cost, or quality.

g.data-narration: Risks are categorized into project, product, business, and technical risks. Each category requires different management approaches.
The risk evaluation process has three main phases: identification, analysis, and prioritization. Each builds upon the previous step.
Common identification techniques include brainstorming, Delphi method, SWOT analysis, and checklist analysis. Each has unique advantages.
Qualitative analysis uses ratings like High/Medium/Low, while quantitative analysis uses numerical values and statistical methods.
Risk Exposure = Probability Impact. This formula calculates the expected loss from a risk and helps prioritize which risks need attention.
g.text: Where: Probability (0-1) x Impact (INR) = Risk Exposure (INR)

g.data-narration: Lets calculate risk exposure for each identified risk. R2 (Technology obsolescence) has the highest exposure at INR 4,00,000.
Expected Monetary Value (EMV) calculates the average outcome when future events are uncertain. It is the sum of probability value for all outcomes.
For mutually exclusive risks, EMV helps decide which risk mitigation to invest in. Total EMV of -INR 1,70,000 shows average expected loss.
g.text: Risk A: 40% probability of INR 2,00,000 loss
Risk B: 60% probability of INR 1,50,000 loss

g.data-narration: Earned Value Management integrates cost and schedule metrics to identify project performance risks. It provides early warning of problems.
g.text: EVM integrates cost and schedule metrics to identify project performance risks:

g.data-narration: Key EVM formulas: CPI = EV/AC (cost efficiency), SPI = EV/PV (schedule efficiency). CPI less than 1 means cost overrun.
In this example, CPI of 0.89 means cost overrun. SPI of 0.80 shows schedule delay. The project needs INR 12,36,000 more to complete.
Five main response strategies: Avoid (change plans), Mitigate (reduce probability/impact), Transfer (to third party), Accept (contingency), Exploit (for opportunities).
Key formulas to remember: Risk Exposure = P I, EMV = Sum(PV), CPI = EV/AC, SPI = EV/PV. These are essential for exams.
Exam tips: Remember CPI less than 1 means cost overrun. Know all response strategies. Risk identification finds risks while evaluation analyzes them.
Risk evaluation is essential for project success. Practice these formulas regularly and understand when to apply each technique for effective risk management.
g.text: Master risk evaluation techniques to become an effective software project manager.
Practice calculating risk exposure, EMV, and EVM metrics regularly!
